variables.content: For the ideal (strictly two-dimensional, zero-width, disorder-free) paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass remains close to unity across the studied density parameters r_s=1, 5, and 10, and the high-precision DMC extrapolations reported here show no evidence for a strong enhancement of m^* at low density in this ideal system.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=1, extrapolating finite-N quasiparticle effective masses m^*(N) produced by quartic fits to DMC energy bands and fitting m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.955(2) in hartree atomic units (a.u.), where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the Fermi wave vector of the infinite system.

The Fermi temperature of the two-dimensional electron gas is calculated from the sheet electron density $n_s$ according to $T_F=\hbar^2\pi n_s/(k_B m^*)$ using the effective mass $m^*=0.19\,m_e$, where $m_e$ is the free-electron mass, and the analysis assumes that this effective mass remains applicable in the dilute low-density regime near the metal-insulator transition.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolating finite-N DMC effective masses obtained from quartic-band fits gives the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.03(4) a.u., where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.

For a two-dimensional electron gas with sheet density n = 1.12×10^{13} cm^{-2} and with electron effective mass m^* = 0.20 m_e (where m_e is the free-electron mass and ħ is the reduced Planck constant), the Fermi energy is given by E_F = π ħ^2 n / m^*, which evaluates numerically to E_F ≈ 2.15×10^{-20} J ≈ 134 meV for the stated n and m^*; since the thermal energy at the upper measurement temperature k_B (80 K) ≈ 6.9 meV is much smaller than E_F, the two-dimensional electron gas is degenerate (E_F >> k_B T) over the interval 0.3 K ≤ T ≤ 80 K under the assumption that m^* ≈ 0.20 m_e applies to the occupied conduction states.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolation of finite-N DMC quartic-fit effective masses yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.70(3) a.u., where m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} for the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=5, the same extrapolation procedure applied to the DMC quartic-fit band data yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.04(2) a.u., with m^* defined as m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.

For the interacting two-dimensional electron gas in the parameter ranges of the samples, the temperature dependence of the interacting effective mass m^*(T) for temperatures satisfying T/T_F ≤ 1 can be represented by the truncated analytical expansion
      m^*/m_e = A(r_s) + B(r_s) (T/T_F) + C(r_s) (T/T_F)^2 ln(T/T_F),
      where A(r_s), B(r_s), and C(r_s) are functions of the interaction parameter r_s taken from many-body theory and T_F = π ħ^2 n_e /(k_B m^*); this expansion is taken to accurately describe the temperature dependence of m^* for the temperatures and densities used in the model–data comparisons.

Using the effective mass m*_{E1}(Γ) = 0.036 m0 (where m0 is the free-electron mass) for electrons in the E1 subband and the experimentally measured two-dimensional carrier density n = 3.2 × 10^12 cm⁻², the two-dimensional Fermi energy of the electron gas was estimated by the authors to be E_F = 88 meV; this estimate is reported to be in close agreement with an independent Fermi-energy value of approximately 91 meV deduced from thermoelectric power measurements.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=5, the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass obtained by extrapolating finite-N DMC quartic-fit results is m^*(∞)=0.74(1) a.u., using m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.
